impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when those products have substantial non-infringing uses beyond the scope of the patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers exclusively purchasing its unpatented carbon honeycomb products, which had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems not covered by the patent). The court held that because the tied products had substantial uses outside the patent's scope, the patent laws did not shield the tying arrangement from antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of patent holders engaged in tying arrangements now have clearer grounds to bring antitrust claims when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses, promoting open competition in component and supply markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that condition patent licenses on purchases of related but unpatented products face significant antitrust exposure. Businesses must carefully evaluate whether tied products have substantial non-infringing uses before structuring exclusive purchase requirements.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls to a model are abstract and ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 at Alice step one, and whether damages can be based on foreign sales of infringing software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that asserted claims of the '322 and '115 patents directed to detecting anomalous program executions were abstract at Alice step one, requiring remand on step two. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales, and reversed the contempt order against Norton's counsel that had supported enhanced damages and attorneys' fees.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against cybersecurity-related patent claims have stronger grounds to challenge such patents as abstract under § 101. The foreign sales ruling limits patentees' ability to recover damages on extraterritorial revenues.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software-monitoring space face heightened § 101 eligibility challenges, potentially undermining the enforceability of patents covering detection and comparison methods.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods and systems for tailoring content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing a jury verdict of infringement and $2.5 million damages award against Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content presentation to device capabilities. The ruling required entry of judgment for Netflix and expressly vacated the district court's rulings on induced infringement and damages evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Major streaming and technology companies gain stronger defenses against patent assertions covering routine content-adaptation and device-tailoring technologies, reducing litigation exposure from non-practicing entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and adaptive streaming space face significant risk that their patents will be found abstract, limiting monetization of inventions related to device-specific content optimization.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and Walker Process antitrust fraud based on alleged concealment of prior art during patent examination.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims related to Tenaris's coiled tubing patents. The court found genuine factual disputes over whether Tenaris concealed prior CYMAX technology documents from the USPTO during prosecution, and whether that concealment supported an attempted monopolization claim. The case was remanded for trial on both issues.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors can pursue both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust counterclaims to trial when patent holders may have concealed material prior art, providing a powerful tool against potentially fraudulently obtained patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face dual exposure to inequitable conduct findings and antitrust liability when material prior art is not disclosed during prosecution, increasing the stakes of patent prosecution decisions.

impacts.caseName: Genuine Enabling Technology LLC v. Sony Group Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patentee's failure to account for all elements of the corresponding structure disclosed in the specification for a means-plus-function claim limitation is fatal to an infringement case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of noninfringement for Sony, finding that GET's infringement analysis of its input device patent failed to account for the full structure disclosed in the specification for a means-plus-function limitation. The court held that because GET and its expert ignored many elements of the logic block identified as the corresponding structure, the infringement case was deficient as a matter of law.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants accused of infringing means-plus-function claims can successfully challenge infringement theories that cherry-pick structural elements, reinforcing the requirement that patentees must map all disclosed corresponding structure to accused products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting means-plus-function claims face a strict requirement to address every element of the disclosed corresponding structure in their infringement analysis, or risk summary judgment of noninfringement.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether web conferencing system claims with time-shifting capabilities enabling asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement suit against Google, holding that claims directed to a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing were ineligible under § 101. The decision reinforces the trend of invalidating software patents that claim conventional computing functions applied to known concepts.
impacts.positiveImplications: Major technology companies benefit from continued judicial skepticism toward patents claiming conventional computing techniques applied to web conferencing and streaming, reducing litigation risk from patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the collaboration and web conferencing technology space face an increasingly difficult landscape for enforcing software-based method and system claims against § 101 challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a district court abused its discretion in excluding a patentee's damages expert report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on the absence of admissible damages evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent infringement case against Microsoft's Azure platform and vacated the resulting summary judgment. The court found the exclusion was an abuse of discretion, holding that alleged deficiencies in the expert's methodology could be addressed through cross-examination rather than wholesale exclusion.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more protective standard for admissibility of damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that methodological disputes result in complete exclusion and case-dispositive summary judgment.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Microsoft may face greater difficulty in obtaining pre-trial exclusion of damages experts, potentially increasing the cost and duration of patent litigation through trial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding two expert witnesses on induced infringement and in granting JMOL of no induced infringement for medical device patents covering en bloc spinal derotation techniques.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed JMOL for DePuy and remanded for a new trial, holding that the district court abused its discretion in excluding both of the patentee's technical and infringement experts. The court found that the experts' testimony was improperly excluded where it was consistent with the court's own claim construction, and that the resulting JMOL was erroneous.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders, particularly individual inventors suing large medical device companies, benefit from appellate scrutiny of expert exclusion orders that effectively decide the case before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face the risk of retrials when courts improperly exclude expert testimony, increasing litigation costs and exposure to induced infringement liability for marketed surgical techniques.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a means-plus-function claim term ('control means') in a crop harvester patent is indefinite when the specification describes the corresponding structure as 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of the '395 patent were invalid as indefinite. The court held that the specification's description of the control means structure as 'conventional in design and operation,' combined with identified hardware components, provided sufficient corresponding structure to inform persons of ordinary skill. The case was remanded for further proceedings on infringement.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from the court's recognition that specification references to 'conventional' structures, when accompanied by identified components, can survive indefiniteness challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural and industrial equipment manufacturers like Deere face continued patent infringement exposure where indefiniteness defenses against means-plus-function claims are narrowly applied.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the jury's verdict of nonobviousness of a pre-lit artificial tree patent and its damages award were supported by substantial evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL on obviousness and motion for new trial on damages for Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with integrated electrical-mechanical connections. The court found substantial evidence supported the jury's finding that the prior art did not render the claimed invention obvious, and that the damages methodology was proper.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with commercially successful products benefit from the court's deference to jury findings on nonobviousness, particularly where the patent addresses a specific technical problem with a novel integrated solution.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies manufacturing competing products face sustained patent infringement liability where obviousness challenges depend on combining disparate prior art references that the jury found insufficient.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the CIT's ruling that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with 19 U.S.C. § 1516a and does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The court treated the ITC's mandamus petition as an appeal under the collateral order doctrine and directed the ITC to comply with statutory confidentiality requirements rather than applying automatic designations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic producers, and the public benefit from greater transparency in trade remedy proceedings, as businesses can access questionnaire data that does not warrant confidential treatment, improving their ability to participate meaningfully in trade litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting business-sensitive information in ITC questionnaires face potential public disclosure of data they previously expected to remain confidential, requiring more careful confidentiality designations and potentially deterring full cooperation with ITC inquiries.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under the APA.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions to the Board on exercising discretion to deny IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court reasoned that because the Director retains ultimate non-institution authority that is insulated from judicial review, the instructions do not bind the Director and are not legislative rules requiring formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued ability to issue flexible guidance on IPR institution decisions without the delay and rigidity of formal rulemaking, preserving the status quo on institution discretion.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners lose a potential avenue to challenge PTO policies that lead to discretionary denials of IPR petitions, as such policies remain insulated from both APA procedural requirements and judicial review.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act when the SBA failed to award grants in the statutorily required order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that over 300 restaurants and businesses stated a viable Tucker Act claim for unpaid Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants. The court held that the statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants' in order of receipt) created an enforceable obligation, and that the SBA's failure to process applications in the required order gave rise to a damages claim even after Congress rescinded remaining funds.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses denied federal grants due to agency processing errors have a viable path to recover damages under the Tucker Act when the authorizing statute uses mandatory language directing the order and manner of disbursement.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability to hundreds of businesses for grants that were never disbursed, establishing that mandatory statutory disbursement language can create enforceable financial obligations even after appropriations are rescinded.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether OPM's regulatory writing requirement for federal employee overtime claims under 5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c) is a valid exercise of delegated rulemaking authority under 5 U.S.C. § 5548(a).
impacts.description: Sitting en banc, the Federal Circuit held that OPM's longstanding regulatory requirement that overtime be authorized in writing is a valid exercise of delegated rulemaking authority. The court affirmed dismissal of Count II of a class action by IHS nurses who claimed they were denied overtime pay during the COVID-19 pandemic, finding the 80-year-old writing requirement consistent with the statutory 'officially ordered or approved' standard. The remaining claims were referred back to the panel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from the validation of the overtime writing requirement, providing a clear administrative mechanism to control overtime expenditures and limit backpay liability for unwritten overtime.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees, particularly healthcare workers who performed substantial unpaid overtime during emergencies, face a significant barrier to recovering overtime pay absent written authorization, even when supervisors were aware of the work.

impacts.caseName: Crocs, Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Section 337 no-violation finding as to active respondents precludes the ITC from issuing a limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents under 19 U.S.C. § 1337(g)(1).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed Crocs's untimely appeal of the ITC's no-violation finding against active respondents but affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order against defaulting respondents. The court held that under § 1337(g)(1), when respondents are found in default, the ITC properly presumes the complaint's allegations are true and issues an LEO, independent of the merits findings as to participating respondents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Brand owners can still obtain limited exclusion orders against defaulting importers even when active respondents successfully defend against infringement claims, preserving some trade remedy protection.
impacts.negativeImplications: Brand owners seeking broad general exclusion orders face limitations when they cannot prove likelihood of confusion or infringement against actively participating respondents, as the no-violation finding constrains the scope of available remedies.
